title: Uterine cancer in rabbits
subtitle: Why uterine adenocarcinoma is so common in entire female rabbits, how it presents, and why early spaying prevents it.
excerpt: Uterine adenocarcinoma is the most common cancer in unspayed female rabbits. Studies suggest over 80% of entire does over four years of age are affected. It is almost entirely preventable by spaying before the age of two. For does not intended for breeding, spaying is standard welfare practice rather than an optional procedure.
sourceNotes: Harcourt-Brown (2002): Textbook of Rabbit Medicine, Butterworth-Heinemann: reproductive disease chapter. RSPCA: spaying and neutering rabbits. Varga (2013), Textbook of Rabbit Medicine (2nd ed.): oncology and reproductive medicine.

glossaryTerms.definition: A malignant tumour originating in the glandular tissue of the rabbit uterus. The most common cancer in entire female rabbits, with a reported prevalence of 50 to 80% in does over four years old depending on breed and study. Spreads to the lungs and liver in advanced cases. Preventable by spaying before the age of two.

A hormonal condition in entire female rabbits where the doe behaves as if pregnant (building a nest, pulling fur, becoming territorial) without being actually pregnant. Triggered by mounting from another rabbit or by manual stimulation. Resolves spontaneously after 16 to 18 days. Repeated pseudopregnancies increase the risk of uterine disease.

content.text: increases steeply after age two in entire does. For a breeding doe used actively for one to two seasons, the practical risk window is understood and the benefits of breeding may be weighed against it. For a doe kept as a companion, fibre producer, or non-breeding meat herd member, the case for spaying before two years of age is straightforward.
Blood in the urine: the most common early sign. Uterine blood passes with the urine and gives it a pink or red tinge. It is often intermittent and may be confused with a urinary tract infection.
A palpable firm mass in the caudal abdomen on routine handling.
Gradual weight loss and reduced activity in a doe over two years.
Lumpy or swollen breasts: breast and uterine tumours often appear at the same time.

Spay at five to six months, once the tract is fully developed but before the disease risk rises. Spaying before five months is possible but the smaller structures increase surgical risk. After age two, spaying is still the right treatment if disease has not spread, but the surgery is harder and the anaesthetic risk is higher.

in entire does is associated with increased uterine disease risk. A doe that has frequent pseudopregnancies (triggered by housing with another intact doe, or by mounting from a neutered male) receives repeated hormonal stimulation to the uterus. This is an additional reason to spay entire does that are not being used for breeding.
